classification: Open-source web server vulnerability scanner (signature- and check-database–driven)
short_description: Nikto is a Perl-based web server scanner that performs tests for dangerous files/CGIs, outdated server software, and common misconfigurations, producing machine- and human-readable reports.
overview_notes: Nikto focuses on identifying known, patternable web server issues rather than deep business-logic or framework-specific flaws. It is maintained on GitHub, offers a plugin-based architecture backed by a checks database, and supports JSON/XML/HTML/CSV reporting. As of February 2026, the project documentation targets version 2.6.0.
what_it_is_notes:
- A command-line scanner for HTTP/HTTPS services, oriented to rapid detection of misconfigurations, default content, known risky resources, and server/software versions that may map to public vulnerabilities.
- Built in Perl and historically built on the LibWhisker HTTP library.
- Commonly used in reconnaissance and baseline assessments; often integrated into automated pipelines or orchestrators to quickly surface low-hanging web server issues.
how_it_works_notes:
- Uses a database of tests executed via plugins to probe HTTP(S) endpoints. Tests include file/directory presence, server option checks, banner and header analysis, default/administrative paths, and version identification.
- The engine performs HTTP(S) requests (optionally through proxies), follows redirects if configured, and can reuse received cookies to continue scanning authenticated or stateful paths.
- Output is generated in several formats to support human review and tooling integration. Updates to code and test data are delivered through the GitHub repository and can be pulled regularly.
inputs: Target hostnames, IPs, or base URLs (HTTP/HTTPS)
Port(s) to scan for HTTP services
outputs: Findings per target with issue type/description
Context such as URL/path, headers, and server banners
Categorization by plugin/test ID and references (where available)
Machine-readable reports (JSON, XML, CSV) and human-readable (TXT/HTML)
core_concepts: Plugin-based architecture governing phases of scanning and reporting
HTTP transaction layer via LibWhisker heritage
Update cadence via GitHub (code and checks)
typical_workflow: Discover live web services and candidate ports, then select in-scope hosts/URLs for baseline scanning.
Run Nikto with appropriate tuning/plugins and safe request options aligned to rules of engagement (rate, redirect handling, cookies, proxies).
Aggregate results in JSON/XML/CSV for ingestion by ticketing, SIEM, or vulnerability management platforms; generate HTML/TXT for human review.
Triage findings by risk and relevance; validate high-signal issues (exposed admin panels, dangerous HTTP methods, outdated server versions).
Correlate version/banner findings with authoritative advisories and CVEs; confirm exploitability through manual or separate tools as appropriate.
Iterate: adjust scope/tuning, retest after remediation, and schedule periodic baseline scans or CI/CD jobs.
use_cases: Baseline reconnaissance of HTTP(S) services to quickly surface misconfigurations and default content.
Periodic hygiene scans of internet-facing web servers for exposed administrative paths, directory indexing, or outdated server components.
Pipeline/automation integration (e.g., in Kubernetes or CI) to produce machine-readable reports for gating and alerting.
Supplementing more comprehensive DAST/proxy-based testing by providing quick server-centric checks and fingerprints.
Validation of control hardening (e.g., disabled TRACE/PUT, header hygiene) and regression checks after patching.
limitations: Coverage is primarily server- and file/endpoint-oriented; it is not designed for deep business-logic testing or full active exploitation.
Findings can include false positives and require analyst validation; some checks are heuristic or banner-based.
Scanning is intentionally noisy and easily detected by defenses; rate-limiting and WAFs may throttle or block requests.
Effectiveness depends on the freshness and breadth of the checks database; it may lag behind commercial scanners in some areas.
Does not replace broader application security testing (e.g., proxy-assisted exploration, authenticated workflow testing, fuzzing).

related_tools.name: OWASP ZAP
related_tools.relationship: Complementary; a full-featured intercepting proxy and active scanner for application-layer testing beyond server checks.

related_tools.name: Nmap
related_tools.relationship: Precursor/adjacent; service discovery and version detection to identify HTTP(S) endpoints prior to Nikto scans.

related_tools.name: Nuclei
related_tools.relationship: Complementary; template-driven scanning for specific vulnerabilities/CVEs across HTTP targets.

related_tools.name: Wapiti
related_tools.relationship: Alternative; open-source web vulnerability scanner with a different test corpus and approach.

related_tools.name: Skipfish
related_tools.relationship: Alternative; high-performance web application security reconnaissance tool.

related_tools.name: Burp Suite
related_tools.relationship: Complementary; manual and automated web testing platform with active scanning and extensibility.

evidence_gaps: Exact, current counts of tests/checks vary by source and version; authoritative counts for 2.6.0 are not consolidated outside the repository and may change with updates.
Historical references sometimes imply OWASP affiliation; Nikto is not an OWASP project, though it is referenced by the OWASP Web Security Testing Guide.
Detailed mapping of each plugin/test to CVE/NVD entries is partial and may require consulting specific test definitions in the repository.
